messages.content:
Select the correct answer.
Read paragraph 6.

(6) Is that, my friends, giving them a fair shake of the dice or anything like the inalienable right of life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness, or anything resembling the fact that all people are created equal; when we have today in America thousands and hundreds of thousands and millions of children on the verge of starvation in a land that is overflowing with too much to eat and too much to wear? I do not think you will contend that, and I do not think for a moment that they will contend it…

Which word best states the meaning of contend as it is used in the paragraph?

 A. 
illustrate
 B. 
study
 C. 
gain
 D. 
claim
D. claim

Select the correct answer.
Read paragraphs 1 to 4 from "Every Man a King."

How does Long use rhetoric to advance his point of view?

 A. 
He uses logos and pathos, evoking the image of starving children and logically arguing that their plight violates the Declaration of Independence.
 B. 
He relies solely on pathos, making an emotional appeal to the plight of poverty-stricken children.
 C. 
He relies solely on ethos, putting forth his experience as a reliable interpreter of the Declaration of Independence.
 D. 
He uses ethos and pathos, asserting his authority as a politician while evoking an emotional response from his audience.
A. He uses logos and pathos, evoking the image of starving children and logically arguing that their plight violates the Declaration of Independence.

Select the correct answer.
Which situation is an example of dramatic irony?

 A. 
The audience knows Orlando is responsible for the poems, but Rosalind does as well.
 B. 
The audience knows Rosalind is wearing a disguise, but Orlando does not. 
 C. 
The audience knows Orlando is in love with Rosalind and is relieved that Rosalind loves him, too.
 D. 
The audience knows Rosalind is accompanied by her cousin, though she speaks no lines in this excerpt.
B. The audience knows Rosalind is wearing a disguise, but Orlando does not.

Select the correct answer.

Which detail introduces the idea that love can be burdensome?

 A. 
Rosalind disguising herself as a shepherd boy
 B. 
Orlando insisting that he can prove that he is in love
 C. 
Orlando wishing for a remedy for his lovesickness
 D. 
Rosalind claiming Orlando does not look like a man in love
C. Orlando wishing for a remedy for his lovesickness

Select the correct answer.

Read line 25 from the scene.

(25) ORLANDO: Fair youth, I would I could make thee believe I love.

What is the effect of this line as a response to Rosalind's description in lines 14–24?

 A. 
It contributes to the tension by revealing how Rosalind feels doubtful of Orlando.
 B. 
It creates a feeling of suspense as the audience is unsure of Orlando's feelings.
 C. 
It confirms for the audience Orlando's genuine feelings for Rosalind.
 D. 
It makes the audience less surprised by Orlando's love for Rosalind.
A. It contributes to the tension by revealing how Rosalind feels doubtful of Orlando.

Select the correct answer from the drop-down menu.

Read the excerpt from the play, then complete the sentence.

ROSALIND: if I could meet that fancy-monger I would

give him some good counsel, for he seems to have the

quotidian of love upon him.

ORLANDO: I am he that is so love-shaked: I pray you tell me

your remedy.



In this excerpt, Shakespeare uses Orlando’s response to create an effect of 
doubt
.
No, the correct answer is humor. Orlando's response is meant to be humorous, as he jokingly refers to himself as "love-shaked" and asks Rosalind for a remedy.

Select the correct answer from the drop-down menu.
Complete the sentence with the correct description of Orlando.

Because Orlando believes he is speaking to Ganymede and not Rosalind, he 
professes his love for Rosalind more freely
.

No, the correct answer is "expresses his lovesickness openly." The fact that Orlando believes he is speaking to Ganymede (and not realizing Ganymede is actually Rosalind) allows him to speak more openly and freely about his love for Rosalind.

Select the correct answer.
How does President Bush use rhetoric to advance the purpose of his speech in paragraph 4?

 A. 
He uses pathos, displaying anger on behalf of the nation.
 B. 
He uses pathos, conveying mourning and eliciting grief.
 C. 
He uses logos, explaining rationally why the attack occurred.
 D. 
He uses ethos, drawing upon his authority as president.
B. He uses pathos, conveying mourning and eliciting grief.

Select the correct answer.
Read this sentence from the speech.

"These acts shatter steel, but they cannot dent the steel of American resolve."

Which phrase best captures the figurative meaning of the phrase the steel of American resolve?

 A. 
the coldness of American actions
 B. 
the perseverance of American people
 C. 
the strength of American determination
 D. 
the courage of American decisions
C. the strength of American determination

Select the correct text in the passage.
Which excerpt of paragraphs 5-6 would be the most useful in an informational paragraph about the word condolences?

(5) I appreciate so very much the members of Congress who have joined me in strongly condemning these attacks. And on behalf of the American people, I thank the many world leaders who have called to offer their condolences and assistance. America and our friends and allies join with all those who want peace and security in the world, and we stand together to win the war against terrorism.

(6) Tonight, I ask for your prayers for all those who grieve, for the children whose worlds have been shattered, for all whose sense of safety and security has been threatened…

The most useful excerpt for an informational paragraph about the word condolences would be "(5) I appreciate so very much the members of Congress who have joined me in strongly condemning these attacks. And on behalf of the American people, I thank the many world leaders who have called to offer their condolences and assistance."

Select the correct answer.
Read this sentence from the speech.

"America was targeted for attack because we're the brightest beacon for freedom and opportunity in the world."

Which word best reflects the connotative meaning of the phrase beacon for freedom and opportunity as it is used in the text?

 A. 
inspiration
 B. 
opportunity
 C. 
society
 D. 
warning
A. inspiration

Think about how speakers must present their message in a way that suits their audience's interests, motivations, or desires in order for it to be effective and achieve their purpose. Consider this scenario:

A student group has been asked to make a presentation at the monthly school board and PTA meeting about moving the start time for school back two hours from 8:00 a.m. to 10:00 a.m.

Which is the most effective way for the student group to present their message to their audience of educators and parents?

They should give real-life examples so the educators and parents can relate to the message.
They should present scientific data that appeals to the educators' and parents' interest in improving education and health.
They should use their own personal experiences to connect with the educators and parents about the topic.
They should emphasize only the health benefits of later start times for teenage students and children.

The most effective way for the student group to present their message to their audience of educators and parents would be to present scientific data that appeals to the educators' and parents' interest in improving education and health.

The State of the Union Address is an annual message delivered by the president of the United States to the US Congress on the current condition of the nation. This speech is also heard by American citizens all over the country. In January of 1941, America was recovering from the Depression of the 1930s. Europe was engaged in World War II, but the United States had not yet entered the war—many Americans did not want to get involved with the conflict.​

Considering the context of FDR's speech, who was probably his intended audience for this speech?

the American people
Congress and European leaders
Congress and the American people
world leaders and the American people
Congress and the American people

Think about the opening lines of FDR's Four Freedoms speech.

What idea does he introduce and begin developing in the beginning of his speech?

America has a long history of war.
There is a threat to American freedom.
Only the Civil War was worth fighting.
America has historically stayed out of foreign conflicts.
The idea that FDR introduces and begins developing in the beginning of his speech is that there is a threat to American freedom.

Reread paragraphs 6 through 9. FDR describes for his audience a series of historical wars that America was involved in. Which of the following best explains why FDR chose to include this information?

to make the case for why America must enter the war in Europe quicky
to show how reluctant America has been historically to become involved in foreign battles
to remind his audience that America has gone to war when democracy was being threatened
to try to connect the German army to past armies America fought against
to remind his audience that America has gone to war when democracy was being threatened

In paragraph 15, FDR states that, "Those who would give up essential liberty to purchase a little temporary safety deserve neither liberty nor safety."

Which of the following best expresses the message FDR intended for his audience?

He is saying that people with unrealistic ideas about war, freedom, and security do not deserve to be free and safe in America.
He is saying that America's freedom of religion and expression is at risk if we let an aggressor like Germany take over our country.
He is saying that expecting an aggressor like Germany to peacefully take over America is naïve and that we will lose the freedoms we take for granted.
He is saying that people who want to stay out of the war do not deserve the freedom or security that going to war would preserve.
He is saying that people who would sacrifice essential liberty for temporary safety do not deserve neither liberty nor safety.

Consider paragraphs 22 through 25.

How does FDR introduce the ideas in these paragraphs to appeal to his audience?

He lists his ideas so they are easy for the audience to understand.
He repeats a phrase that suggests most Americans agree with him.
He provides specific details and descriptions of the national policy.
He uses repetition because it can help people remember what he says.
He uses repetition because it can help people remember what he says.

Consider paragraphs 37 through 47. What is FDR's message to the nation in this section?

Select the correct answer from the drop-down menu.

FDR's message to the nation is 
that we should prepare ourselves for the possibility of getting involved in the war in Europe.

No, that is not the correct answer. FDR's message to the nation in this section is that America must become the "arsenal of democracy" and provide support to countries fighting against aggression and tyranny.

Look again at paragraphs 71–72.

How does FDR draw a connection between the new order and the moral order to achieve his purpose?

He believes the dictators of the new order will use bombs to try to stop the moral order from prevailing.
He makes the point that the old order is fearful of the new order, which is why the United States must act.
He compares the new order—representing tyranny—and the moral order—representing democracy—to convey that Americans should fight to preserve democracy.
He suggests that the new order—representing tyranny—is a distant possibility to convey that the moral order—representing democracy—is possible now.
He compares the new order—representing tyranny—and the moral order—representing democracy—to convey that Americans should fight to preserve democracy.
